title: Registry never reads or validates phases.json — contract mismatch
description: The scanner reads and validates path.json only. The contract for registry.ts states it 'Skips non-directories, malformed JSON, schema-invalid files, and dirs missing path.json' — implying phases.json is part of what makes a path 'well-formed'. validatePhases() is exported and unit-tested directly (T-037..T-039), but the registry loop never calls it. A path dir with a valid path.json and a malformed or schema-invalid phases.json will surface as a healthy PathInfo with zero warnings.
impact: Cycle 4 (the only consumer of phase semantics) will receive PathInfos that the engine vouched for, then crash when it tries to load phases. The 'parametricity' guarantee in A6 is weaker than advertised: dropping a path dir with a broken phases.json gets quiet acceptance instead of a structured warning. The contract's 'phases.json schema-only validation' obligation is not enforced anywhere observable.
recommendation: Inside the per-entry loop (after path.json validates), read paths.join(slugDir, 'phases.json'), parse, run validatePhases. On read/parse/schema failure emit a structured warning ('missing-phases-json' | 'malformed-phases-json' | 'invalid-phases-json') and skip the entry, mirroring the path.json logic.

title: Registry trusts slug from path.json without checking dir name match
description: scanRegistry iterates directory entries by entry.name but emits PathInfo.slug from validation.value.slug (the value inside path.json). If a dir is named '01-orderbook-viewer' but its path.json declares slug 'something-else', the registry happily reports slug 'something-else'. The dir-name vs slug-field invariant the contract implies ('paths/<slug>/path.json') is unverified.
impact: Cycle 2+ tools that resolve a slug back to a filesystem dir (e.g. to load phases or run a build_command) cannot rely on the slug to find the dir. Two paths with the same internal slug across different dirs would dedupe weirdly downstream. No current test catches this because T-019 fixture and T-020 fixture both have matching dir names.
recommendation: After validatePath succeeds, assert validation.value.slug === entry.name; on mismatch emit a structured warning ('slug-dir-mismatch') with both values and skip the entry, OR canonicalize on entry.name and treat the path.json slug field as advisory.

title: scanRegistry conflates ENOENT with other readdir errors as 'no-paths-dir'
description: The catch block returns kind 'no-paths-dir' for ANY error from readdirSync — including EACCES (permission denied), ENOTDIR (path is a file), EMFILE (file table full). Only ENOENT semantically matches 'absent paths directory' per A9. A path that exists but is unreadable will be reported as absent, hiding the real cause from the user.
impact: User runs /sui-deepbook-course:start, sees 'no paths installed', and assumes they need to clone path content — when actually their paths/ dir has wrong permissions and is unreadable. Diagnosis loop is broken. The empty-paths-dir vs no-paths-dir distinction in A8/A9 was specifically introduced to make this discoverable; ENOENT-only handling preserves it.
recommendation: Inspect err.code: ENOENT → 'no-paths-dir'; ENOTDIR → 'paths-not-a-directory'; everything else → 'paths-read-error' with the underlying message. T-025 only exercises the ENOENT path so this is currently green by coincidence.

title: outputStyle probe collapses ENOENT and read-error into 'settings-file-missing'
description: Any failure of fs.readFileSync — EACCES, EISDIR, EMFILE — is reported as 'settings-file-missing'. The kind suggests an absent file; the underlying cause may be a permission error on a present file. The error object is discarded (catch with no binding), so users have no signal to diagnose.
impact: If ~/.claude/settings.json exists but is unreadable for the running process, the user sees 'settings file not found' and is told to enable a plugin that may already be enabled. This is a soft correctness regression of the contract's 'structured note' obligation in line 17 of contract.md ('returns false with a structured note').
recommendation: catch (err) and inspect err.code; emit kind 'settings-file-missing' only on ENOENT, otherwise 'settings-file-unreadable' with the OS-level message. T-015 specifies a missing-file fixture so it would still pass; an additional unreadable-file scenario is not currently covered.

title: validatePath accepts empty-string title, summary, build_command
description: Slug is checked for both type AND non-empty length (line 26). Title, summary, and build_command are checked only for type 'string' — an empty string slips through as valid. T-019 asserts orderbook.title.length > 0 from the real path.json fixture (which has a non-empty title), so the gap is invisible to the test suite but a malformed authored path could surface a PathInfo with title === ''.
impact: Consumer rendering ('Available Paths: <title> — <summary>') would print blanks. The skill's UX assumes non-empty fields. Contract A5/A7 imply schema rejection for malformed path.json; whether 'title: ""' counts as malformed is ambiguous, but the slug treatment establishes a precedent of 'string + non-empty' for required text fields.
recommendation: Apply the same 'typeof X === string && X.length > 0' check to title, summary, and build_command for parity with the slug check. Update the corresponding error messages to identify the empty-string case distinctly from a missing field.

title: outputStyle silently accepts when enabledPlugins is an array
description: The type guard checks typeof === 'object' && !== null but does not exclude arrays. enabledPlugins as an array (e.g. user wrote a list instead of a map) passes the guard; the subsequent index access enabledPlugins['learning-output-style@claude-plugins-official'] returns undefined; the function returns ok=false with no warning. The malformed-data case is silently equated with 'plugin disabled'.
impact: User who mis-edits settings.json into an array shape sees no diagnostic — the course just acts as if the output style is off. Compared to the JSON-parse-error case (which DOES warn), this is an inconsistent treatment of malformed-but-parseable settings. Not a critical defect, but the silent-disable semantics in A4/T-017 are intended for the 'key absent' scenario, not 'key present but wrong shape'.
recommendation: Tighten the guard: typeof === 'object' && !== null && !Array.isArray(...). If enabledPlugins exists but has the wrong shape, emit a 'settings-file-malformed' warning. T-017's silent-disable case (enabledPlugins entirely absent) stays unaffected.
evidence:
typeof (parsed as Record<string, unknown>)['enabledPlugins'] !== 'object' ||
    (parsed as Record<string, unknown>)['enabledPlugins'] === null

title: Registry uses fs.existsSync for path.json; race-prone vs structured stat
description: fs.existsSync is the recommended-against probe — it returns false on ANY error (ENOENT, EACCES, broken symlink). On a permission-restricted path.json the registry will report 'missing-path-json' even though the file exists. Also, existsSync followed by readFileSync is a TOCTOU pattern (irrelevant in single-user CLI but still antipattern). Contract A7 names a 'malformed' / 'missing' distinction; conflating unreadable with missing breaks that distinction.
impact: User-visible warning misidentifies the cause; debugging requires running stat manually. Not a green-test failure because no fixture exercises a permission-denied path.json.
recommendation: Replace existsSync with try/readFileSync; on ENOENT emit 'missing-path-json', on other errors emit a distinct 'unreadable-path-json' warning carrying the OS-level error message.

title: validatePath silently allows duplicate personalization_options entries
description: personalization_options is validated element-by-element against the enum, but no check rejects ['poll_interval_ms','poll_interval_ms']. The downstream personalization layer (cycle 3+) presumably treats the option as a flag; duplicates are harmless but indicate the authored path.json is malformed in a way the schema does not flag.
impact: No runtime breakage in cycle 1; future personalization renderer may or may not be idempotent under duplicates. Surfacing this now is cheap; surfacing it later may require schema migration.
recommendation: After the enum check, assert new Set(opts).size === opts.length; if not, return ok=false with an error naming the duplicate. Cheap, deterministic, and prevents content-authoring bugs from sneaking through.
evidence:
for (const opt of obj['personalization_options'] as unknown[]) {
    if (typeof opt !== 'string' || !VALID_PERSONALIZATION_OPTIONS.has(opt)) {
